_patch_meta.session_ref: M11 Phase 2 Pass A meaning record via Claude API
_patch_meta.source: Claude API meaning record (Sonnet 4.6); 287 verses across 14 terms

set.analysis_note: God's inner being is moved to deep sorrow over creating humanity, the grief penetrating to his heart as he witnesses pervasive human wickedness.
God declares that inner sorrow over making humanity drives his resolve to undo creation, the relenting becoming an active turning away.
The speaker calculates that a gift sent ahead may soften the other's inner disposition and secure acceptance, using appeasement as a strategy to cover relational guilt.
God anticipates that the people's inner resolve will collapse at the sight of war, causing them to mentally change course and turn back, so he redirects their path to prevent that relenting.
Atonement ritually transforms those consecrated, and only they may eat the holy food, showing that the inner status of being set apart is effected through the atoning act.
A uniform ransom offering — regardless of social standing — atones for every person's life, showing that guilt before God knows no class distinction.
The collected ransom money keeps the people in God's remembrance and effects ongoing atonement for their lives, linking material act to sustained inner standing before God.
The intercessor appeals for God to turn from burning anger and relent from punishment, treating divine relenting as a moral turning of the inner will away from just wrath.
set.keywords: ["relenting appealed", "anger turning", "wrath arrested", "intercession urging", "will divine redirected"]

set.analysis_note: God actually relents from the announced disaster, demonstrating that divine inner disposition is genuinely responsive and the sentence of judgment is reversed.
set.keywords: ["relenting enacted", "judgment reversed", "wrath withdrawn", "will divine responsive", "disaster averted"]

set.analysis_note: Moses acknowledges the people's great sin and rises to intercede, hoping that a mediating act of atonement might cover the corporate guilt before God.
The petitioner confesses the people's stubbornness and pleads for divine pardon of iniquity and sin, appealing to God's gracious character to forgive and reclaim the wayward.
The offerer's hand-laying transfers identification with the animal, and the sacrifice is accepted to make atonement, covering the person's guilt before God through ritual substitution.
Priestly atonement mediates between the offerer's guilt and God, and the outcome is that those for whom atonement is made are declared forgiven, guilt fully resolved.
Following priestly atonement, the congregation receives divine forgiveness — the inner burden of guilt is lifted by God's declarative word of release.
The priest's atoning act covers the individual's sin, and the person is released from guilt — atonement produces a restored inner standing before God.
Forgiveness follows completed atonement, signaling the inner guilt's removal and the person's full restoration to right standing before God.
Through sacrifice and priestly mediation, atonement is made and the offerer forgiven, the pleasing aroma signaling divine acceptance of the guilt-covering act.
The forgiveness granted after atonement confirms that the inner burden of sin is fully discharged, leaving the person clean before God.
Priestly atonement for a specifically committed sin results in forgiveness, showing the ritual's power to address even a known, particular act of inner failure.
Once atonement is complete, the person is forgiven — the verdict of release from a specific sin's guilt is granted by God through the mediating priest.
set.keywords: ["forgiveness declared", "specific guilt released", "verdict granted", "sin resolved", "atonement outcome"]

set.analysis_note: The offender brings a required animal as compensation for sin, and priestly atonement covers that guilt — the sacrifice is the outer act addressing inner culpability.
set.keywords: ["guilt compensated", "sin offering brought", "culpability addressed", "atonement covering", "priest mediating"]

set.analysis_note: A two-bird offering completes atonement for committed sin, and forgiveness is the result — guilt is removed through graduated, accessible ritual means.
Forgiveness is the declared outcome when priestly atonement covers committed sin, showing that guilt before God is fully released through the completed ritual.
Atonement covers any sin from a list of offenses, and forgiveness is the result — the ritual's scope is broad, addressing multiple forms of inner moral failure.
set.keywords: ["atonement broad", "multiple sins covered", "guilt resolved", "forgiveness resulting", "moral failure addressed"]

set.analysis_note: Forgiveness is granted after atonement for any of several sins committed, confirming that the inner guilt from varied moral failures is fully discharged.
set.keywords: ["forgiveness granted", "varied guilt discharged", "sin covered", "conscience cleared", "atonement outcome"]

set.analysis_note: Restitution plus a guilt offering enables priestly atonement for desecrating holy things; restitution addresses the outer wrong while atonement resolves the inner guilt.
Forgiveness follows priestly atonement after restitution is made, showing that full resolution of inner guilt requires both material repair and ritual covering.
Even an unintentional mistake incurs real guilt requiring priestly atonement, and forgiveness follows — inner culpability exists independent of conscious intent.
Forgiveness is granted for an unintentional error once atonement is made, affirming that even unconscious guilt is fully removable through priestly mediation.
set.keywords: ["forgiveness granted", "unintentional guilt removed", "atonement outcome", "conscience cleared", "mistake covered"]

set.analysis_note: Priestly atonement before God releases the person from guilt incurred through any of several actions, and forgiveness is declared — the ritual covers the full range of culpable acts.
Forgiveness is the outcome for whoever has incurred guilt through sinful action, granted through priestly atonement before God — guilt from any qualifying act is fully resolved.
A sin offering whose blood enters the sanctuary cannot be eaten but must be burned, marking the most solemn level of atonement where even the vehicle of covering is wholly consumed.
set.keywords: ["atonement solemn", "holiness boundary", "sacrifice consumed", "guilt most serious", "sanctuary atonement"]

set.analysis_note: The guilt offering and sin offering follow the same law, showing that atonement — whatever the precise violation — operates through the same mediating structure.
The Lord commands the ordination ceremonies to be repeated daily, each day's rites making atonement for those being consecrated — ongoing ritual covering of the priests' inner guilt.
set.keywords: ["atonement ongoing", "consecration requiring", "guilt covered daily", "obedience commanded", "priest prepared"]

set.analysis_note: The high priest is commanded to offer atonement first for himself and then for the people, showing that the mediator's own inner guilt must be resolved before he can cover others' guilt.
set.keywords: ["self atonement first", "guilt personal", "mediation ordered", "priest cleansed", "people covered"]

set.analysis_note: The sin offering is most holy precisely because it bears the congregation's iniquity — eating it is part of the priestly act of carrying and covering collective moral guilt.
After childbirth, priestly atonement cleanses the woman from ritual uncleanness, restoring her inner and relational standing before God.
set.keywords: ["atonement cleansing", "uncleanness covered", "standing restored", "ritual impurity addressed", "priest mediating"]

set.analysis_note: A provision for the poor — two birds instead of a lamb — ensures that atonement is accessible to all regardless of means, and the woman is fully cleansed.
Oil applied to the restored person's head precedes atonement, the anointing and priestly act together covering whatever guilt or defilement required cleansing.
The sin offering for the person being cleansed from skin disease first addresses uncleanness through atonement, with the burnt offering following — guilt is addressed before complete restoration.
set.keywords: ["atonement prior", "uncleanness addressed", "guilt covered", "restoration sequential", "sin offering required"]

set.analysis_note: Burnt offering and grain offering together complete the priestly atonement, and the result is that the formerly defiled person is declared clean — full inner and ritual restoration.
A reduced-cost offering for a poor person still accomplishes full atonement, showing that economic status does not diminish access to guilt-covering and restoration.
Oil on the head of the one being cleansed, applied by the priest, is part of the atonement before God — the anointing signifies the person's covered and restored standing.
set.keywords: ["atonement signified", "anointing covering", "standing restored", "priest mediating", "cleansing before god"]

set.analysis_note: The graduated offering — whatever the person can afford — is presented for atonement before God, ensuring the cleansed person's inner guilt is fully covered regardless of wealth.
set.keywords: ["atonement before god", "guilt covered", "offering proportional", "cleansing completed", "priest mediating"]

set.analysis_note: Two birds offered as sin and burnt offerings enable priestly atonement for a man's bodily discharge, covering the ritual guilt that his unclean condition incurred before God.
set.keywords: ["atonement bodily", "discharge guilt", "uncleanness covered", "priest mediating", "ritual guilt resolved"]

set.analysis_note: Priestly atonement before God covers the woman's unclean discharge, restoring her to clean standing — ritual defilement and its associated guilt are addressed through the offerings.
Before interceding for others, the high priest must first atone for his own guilt and that of his household — self-purification precedes communal mediation.
set.keywords: ["self atonement", "guilt personal", "household covered", "mediation prepared", "priest cleansed first"]

set.analysis_note: The live goat presented before God carries the atonement that will be accomplished over it before being sent away, symbolically bearing guilt out of the community.
set.keywords: ["guilt bearing", "atonement over", "removal symbolic", "scapegoat presented", "sin carried away"]

set.analysis_note: The high priest offers atonement for his own sin before entering the holiest space, his own guilt needing priestly covering before he can mediate for the whole community.
set.keywords: ["self atonement required", "personal guilt covered", "household included", "priest prepared", "mediation prerequisite"]

set.analysis_note: Atonement is made for the sanctuary itself, defiled by the accumulated uncleanness, transgressions, and sins of the entire people — collective moral guilt has even spatial consequences requiring ritual repair.
set.keywords: ["atonement communal", "guilt collective", "transgression accumulated", "sanctuary defiled", "sin spatial impact"]

set.analysis_note: Atonement must be accomplished in complete solitude — the priest bears the weight of guilt for himself, his household, and the whole assembly, with no other human presence allowed during the sacred act.
Ritual purification and sacrifice together enact atonement for both priest and people, showing that the mediator must first be cleansed before he can cover the guilt of others.
Atonement on the Day of Atonement achieves inner cleansing from all sins, restoring the person to a state of purity before God — the guilt is fully removed, not merely managed.
The consecrated priest, clothed in holy garments, is the designated agent who performs atonement — his office and vestments signal that covering guilt requires authorized, set-apart mediation.

Atonement extends outward in concentric circles — sanctuary, altar, priests, all the people — showing that guilt contaminates every layer of communal and sacred life and must be covered at each level.
Annual atonement is instituted as a permanent statute, acknowledging the recurring accumulation of the people's sins and the ongoing need for guilt to be covered before God.
Blood is the divinely appointed means of atonement because life itself resides in it — guilt lodged in the soul is covered by the substitution of one life given for another.
Priestly atonement via a guilt offering removes the specific sin committed and results in divine forgiveness — the act of wrongdoing is both acknowledged and formally covered.
Following atonement for a specific committed sin, divine forgiveness is declared — the guilt is not only covered ritually but released by God, restoring the offender's standing.
All work must cease on the Day of Atonement because the entire day is devoted to the singular act of covering the people's guilt before God — human striving yields to divine covering.
Where interpersonal restitution cannot be made to a human party, the atonement offering still completes the moral resolution — guilt demands satisfaction even when the relational channel is closed.
Inadvertent defilement through contact with the dead incurs guilt that requires atonement before consecration can resume — even unintentional moral contamination must be covered.
The Levites' hands on the bulls enact identification with the sacrifice; atonement is made for them as a group, covering whatever guilt would otherwise bar them from serving before God.
The Levites' role as mediators — making atonement for the people — functions as a protective barrier, preventing the divine wrath that guilt would otherwise provoke when the people draw near.
The Levites' own self-purification from sin and the accompanying atonement ritual shows that those who mediate guilt for others must themselves be cleansed first.
Intercession appeals to God's steadfast love as the basis for pardoning communal iniquity — forgiveness is sought not on merit but on the character of the one asked to forgive.
God grants pardon immediately in response to the intercessor's plea, showing that divine forgiveness is a sovereign act of the will responding to intercession.
Atonement for an unintentional communal sin, accompanied by the proper offering, results in the congregation being forgiven — the mistake is acknowledged and covered through prescribed means.
Forgiveness follows atonement for a communal mistake — the congregation's guilt from inadvertent sin is fully released once the ritual offering is brought.
Forgiveness extends to every person in the community, including resident foreigners, because the mistake was shared — collective inadvertent guilt brings collective forgiveness.
The priest makes atonement specifically for the individual who sinned unintentionally, and forgiveness follows — inadvertent violation is covered when the proper mediation is performed.
After priestly atonement for unintentional sin, the individual is forgiven — the guilt of inadvertent wrongdoing is cleared through the appointed mediatorial act.
Atonement via incense is urgently deployed to halt divine wrath already unleashed in plague — the mediatorial act stands between accumulated guilt and its deadly consequence.
The priest runs into the dying assembly and makes atonement, physically interposing between the people and plague — the act of covering guilt arrests the outworking of divine judgment.
God, unlike man, never changes his mind (relents) because he does not lie — the inner faculty of relenting or reconsidering belongs to human nature, not to divine constancy.
Zealous action that makes atonement for the people is rewarded with a perpetual covenant — the inner motive of jealousy for God translates into a covering act that averts wrath.
A male goat is prescribed as a sin offering for atonement — the recurring sacrificial provision acknowledges that guilt must be regularly covered through appointed means.
A male goat for atonement is again prescribed within the festival cycle, reiterating that guilt accumulated over the appointed period requires a specific covering sacrifice.
A male goat for a sin offering is prescribed to make atonement, once again marking that each sacred assembly carries its attendant need for guilt to be covered before God.
When a father nullifies his daughter's vow, God forgives her any obligation — the guilt of a broken vow is released because her autonomy was overridden by proper authority.
A husband's annulment of a thoughtless vow removes his wife's binding obligation, and God forgives her — rash words that bind the inner person are released through legitimate authority.
When a husband voids his wife's vow on hearing it, whatever she pledged is cancelled and God forgives her — binding self-commitments are dissolved and the accompanying guilt is removed.
Warriors bring personal valuables as an offering to make atonement for themselves before God — their inner awareness of guilt from battle prompts voluntary propitiatory action.
The command to 'lay it to your heart' calls for inward cognitive-volitional turning — returning to awareness of God's sole sovereignty is a deliberate act of the heart and mind.
The prayer calls on God to accept atonement for the guilt of innocent blood shed among the people — corporate bloodguilt requires divine absolution that the community actively seeks.
God will not forgive the one who, hearing the covenant's curses, secretly blesses himself — wilful self-deception in the face of known obligation places the person beyond divine pardon.
Amid exile, when blessing and curse are recalled inwardly, the heart's act of calling them to mind is the inner beginning of return — memory of the covenant initiates repentance.
God's cleansing of his people's land is the culminating act of atonement at a national scale — it is a divine, not human, covering that wipes away corporate guilt from the land itself.
The house's iniquity is declared permanently beyond atonement by sacrifice — the guilt is so entrenched and unrepented that no ritual covering can reach it, leaving it exposed forever.
God expresses regret (na.cham) over having made a king who turned away from obedience — divine sorrow here reflects a relational response to human wilful defection.
The Glory of Israel does not relent or change his mind the way a human would — divine constancy is contrasted with the human capacity for regret and reversal of purpose.
The Lord regrets having made the king after the king's persistent disobedience — divine grief over a human's failure represents a relational sorrow at irreversible moral collapse.
The king seeks to know what atonement is required to repair a broken relationship and restore blessing — guilt from a past wrong compels active searching for the means of reconciliation.
God relents from the calamity mid-execution, stopping the destroying angel — divine compassion overrides declared judgment when the inner grief of relenting moves God to mercy.
Forgiveness is what God is petitioned to do when he hears prayer directed toward his dwelling — the whole act of prayer is oriented toward receiving divine pardon.
Hearing and forgiving the people's sin is paired with restoring them to their land — forgiveness is not only relational but restorative, reversing the consequences of guilt.
Forgiveness of sin is joined to God's teaching the good way — divine pardon opens the person to moral instruction, linking guilt-release to renewed orientation of life.
God is asked to forgive with full knowledge of each person's heart — forgiveness here requires divine heart-knowledge, since only God can truly assess inner motive and render just pardon.
In captivity, the inward act of turning the heart, acknowledging sin, and pleading with God is presented as the beginning of genuine return — repentance begins with a heart-level recognition.
God is petitioned to forgive all transgressions committed, releasing both guilt and its social consequences by moving captors to show compassion — forgiveness has external as well as inner effects.
A servant anticipates moral conflict—compelled by duty to bow in an idol's temple—and pleads preemptively for forgiveness, revealing conscience awareness of an unavoidable compromise.
The shedding of innocent blood accumulates guilt so severe that forgiveness is withheld entirely, showing a boundary beyond which pardon is refused.
Priestly atonement rituals are presented as the divinely appointed mechanism for removing corporate guilt, mediating between a holy God and a sinful people.
God observes the destruction underway and relents, withdrawing further judgment—showing that divine mercy can override purposed wrath when a limit is perceived.
Prayer directed toward God's dwelling is presented as the act that moves God to forgive, linking honest petition to the release of pardon.
Forgiveness of national sin is tied to God hearing prayer, and its result is restoration to the land—pardon and return bound together.
Forgiveness of communal sin is coupled with divine instruction in right conduct, showing that pardon includes moral reorientation toward obedience.
God's forgiveness is grounded in his unique knowledge of each person's heart, making pardon an act of intimate divine discernment rather than merely external judgment.
Inward turning of the heart in exile—accompanied by confession of sin—is the inner act of repentance that precedes and enables restoration.
God is asked to hear the prayers of the exiled and forgive those who have sinned, linking intercession, guilt, and the hope of pardon in captivity.
Forgiveness follows a sequence of inner dispositions—humility, prayer, seeking, and turning—showing that pardon is conditioned on a specific inward movement.
A ritual sin offering is made on behalf of the entire people, showing atonement as a corporate act that removes collective guilt through priestly sacrifice.
A prayer for divine pardon is offered for those who ate the Passover in a ritually impure state, trusting that sincere intent can be covered by God's goodness.
Despite Israel's persistent stiff-necked rebellion, God's nature as one 'ready to forgive' is his defining inner disposition—mercy not exhausted by repeated failure.
Ongoing sin offerings are listed as regular provisions for atonement, presenting guilt removal as a continual institutional need of the community.
Deep self-abasement and repentance in dust and ashes reveal an inner collapse of self-regard before God—genuine, whole-person contrition.
The psalmist appeals for pardon not on personal merit but on God's own name, while acknowledging the weight of great personal guilt.
When overwhelming iniquities exceed the person's capacity to manage them, God himself acts to atone—placing atonement entirely in divine initiative.
Divine compassion moves God to atone for iniquity and restrain wrath repeatedly, showing mercy as an inner disposition overriding deserved punishment.
Atonement for sin is pleaded as an act of divine glory—the removal of guilt becomes an expression of God's own honor and saving character.
God's readiness to forgive is presented as a stable inner character trait—'forgiving' is who God is, not just what he occasionally does.
Forgiveness of all iniquity is listed as a defining benefit of God's relationship to the person—comprehensive, not selective, pardon.
God relents from judgment by remembering his covenant, moved by the abundance of his steadfast love—mercy rising from covenant loyalty.
The Lord swears an oath he will not retract or relent from—here relenting is negated, signaling irrevocable divine commitment rather than a change of heart.
Despite being ensnared by the wicked, the speaker does not abandon or return from adherence to God's law—loyalty holds under external pressure.
The existence of forgiveness with God is itself the basis for reverent fear—pardon does not diminish awe but grounds it.
Iniquity is atoned for through the inner dispositions of steadfast love and faithfulness, not merely ritual—the moral character of the person is the atonement mechanism.
A wise person acts to appease royal wrath before it strikes—here atonement operates as interpersonal prudence, defusing dangerous anger.
A divine touch removes guilt and atones for sin in a direct, transforming act—the inner burden of guilt is lifted by sacred intervention.
Guilt is declared unforgivable until death—atonement is explicitly denied, marking the inner state as sealed against any possibility of restoration.
Full atonement for national guilt comes through the complete removal of idolatry—the inner fruit of repentance is measured by total renunciation of false worship.
A false covenant with death will be annulled—the atonement or covering people thought they secured is exposed as void, leaving them without protection.
The idol-maker lacks the inner reflection to recognize the absurdity of worshipping what remains from firewood—moral blindness prevents any return to reason.
Transgressors are commanded to recall truth to mind and stand firm—an appeal to memory and inner resolve as the path away from rebellion.
Coming disaster cannot be charmed away or atoned for—the inability to atone signals that the inner resources for dealing with guilt are entirely exhausted.
Abundant pardon awaits the wicked who forsake their ways and return to God—forgiveness is portrayed as generous and unconditional upon genuine turning.
God questions whether he should relent for idolatrous offerings poured out to false gods—implying that such betrayal removes the basis for divine sympathy.
God declares he has purposed judgment and will not relent—divine will is fixed and no inner change will alter the announced course of action.
The search for even one just person who seeks truth is the condition on which God would extend pardon—radical scarcity of righteousness blocks forgiveness.
God asks how he can pardon a people who, despite his provision, abandoned him for idols and immorality—ingratitude and repeated betrayal make forgiveness rhetorically impossible.
No person relents of their evil or questions their conduct—inner self-examination is entirely absent, revealing a conscience that does not function to prompt turning.
God says he is weary of relenting—repeated mercy extended to a persistently rebellious people has reached exhaustion, and further patience is withdrawn.
If a nation turns from evil, God will relent of planned disaster—showing divine responsiveness to genuine inner moral change.
If a nation does evil and refuses to obey, God will relent of promised good—divine mercy is equally contingent on moral response in both directions.
The prophet pleads that the plotters' iniquity not be atoned for—a deliberate prayer that atonement be denied, holding guilt permanently in divine sight.
The possibility that the people will hear and turn from evil motivates God's warning—relenting from disaster is conditioned on genuine inner repentance.
Mending ways and obeying God are presented as the acts that will cause God to relent—outward conduct rooted in inner reorientation removes threatened judgment.
Past relenting in response to fear and entreaty is cited as precedent—showing that sincere seeking of God moved divine compassion and averted disaster.
After turning away the speaker relents—shame, instruction, and self-reproach produce genuine inner grief over past sin, marking deep personal contrition.
Under the new covenant, God will forgive iniquity completely and remember sin no more—pardon is total, removing guilt from divine memory permanently.
Divine forgiveness here operates as a double act of cleansing and pardoning: guilt from sin and rebellion is both wiped away and released, restoring the relationship severed by moral failure.
Forgiveness is held out as a conditional possibility contingent on hearing and turning; the inner act of repentance unlocks the release of iniquity and sin.
Divine relenting is presented as a change of disposition in response to the people's situation, withdrawing the execution of disaster and signaling renewed intent to build rather than destroy.
Forgiveness here is so complete that iniquity and sin become unfindable—the remnant is pardoned to the point of moral slate-clearing, with no trace of guilt remaining.
The act of recalling something to mind becomes the pivot that generates hope amid grief; inner memory is redirected to produce forward-oriented feeling rather than despair.
Confession of transgression and rebellion stands alongside the stark acknowledgment that forgiveness has not been granted, making the absence of pardon a felt inner wound.
Atonement produces shame and silencing in the inner person—the experience of being covered despite past unfaithfulness closes the mouth and opens the memory to lasting confounded humility.
Divine refusal to relent is absolute here—no sparing, no reversal—judgment proceeds with finality, the opposite inner state to compassionate relenting.

Ritual sacrifice functions as the structural mechanism of atonement, covering the community before God through prescribed offering; the inner-being content is communal guilt addressed by priestly mediation.
The prince bears the duty of providing atonement offerings on behalf of the whole community, making ongoing atonement a structural responsibility that addresses collective guilt at appointed times.
Forgiveness is acknowledged as belonging to God's very nature, and the confession of rebellion makes the appeal credible—mercy and forgiveness are the ground of hope despite guilt.
Urgent, repeated petition for forgiveness is driven not by merit but by God's own name—the inner posture is desperate pleading with appeal to divine identity rather than human worthiness.

Atonement for iniquity is one element of a cosmic resolution that ends transgression and sin permanently, situating the inner act of covering within an eschatological framework of total moral cleansing.
Divine relenting over disaster is grounded in God's character—gracious, merciful, slow to anger—making inner relenting a natural expression of steadfast love rather than arbitrary change.
Relenting is held open as a hopeful possibility contingent on divine freedom—the question 'who knows?' frames divine compassion as neither guaranteed nor ruled out, evoking humble expectation.
Intercession for forgiveness is driven by the smallness and vulnerability of the people—the prophet's inner cry appeals to mercy on behalf of those who cannot stand on their own.
Divine relenting in response to intercession cancels an announced judgment, showing that compassionate inner movement reverses declared disaster when pleading is heard.
A second divine relenting mirrors the first, reinforcing that God's compassion can be moved by intercession to withdraw announced punishment, even repeatedly.
Relenting is held as an open possibility motivating repentance—the hope that God may turn from anger to mercy drives the inner act of turning toward God.
God's relenting is directly triggered by observing the people's genuine turning from evil—inner change in the people produces a responsive inner change in divine disposition, averting disaster.
Relenting from disaster is cited as a known divine character trait—the prophet's frustration reveals that God's compassionate relenting was anticipated and even resented as too predictable.
Past refusal to relent is presented as purposeful resolve—when provoked to wrath, the decision to bring disaster was carried through without inner retreat, establishing contrast with future blessing.
set.keywords: ["relenting refused", "wrath purposeful", "resolve fixed", "disaster executed", "mercy withheld past"]

set.analysis_note: Permission to proceed is framed as fitting obedience to righteousness—the releasing/permitting here carries no forgiveness content but is an act of willing consent to fulfil a moral purpose.
Leaving nets is an act of volitional release—the disciples detach from their means of livelihood in immediate response to a call, the inner act being decisive will-surrender.
Leaving both boat and father signals an even more radical inner detachment—family bonds and livelihood are simultaneously released in a single act of will following the call.
Leaving the gift at the altar to be reconciled first places relational repair above religious duty, showing that inner reconciliation with a brother takes priority over outward worship.
Permitting one who sues to take even more than demanded is an act of inner non-resistance, releasing attachment to possessions and forgoing the instinct of self-protection.
set.keywords: ["non resistance", "attachment released", "self protection abandoned", "will yielding", "generosity radical"]

set.analysis_note: The prayer links receiving forgiveness for one's own moral debts to having already forgiven others, making the inner act of forgiving debtors the precondition mirrored in petitioning God.
Forgiving others' trespasses is presented as the condition that opens the door to receiving divine forgiveness—the inner act of releasing grievance activates corresponding divine release.
Refusing to forgive others' trespasses blocks one's own forgiveness—a closed heart toward others produces a closed divine response, making inner withholding of pardon self-damaging.
set.keywords: ["forgiveness withheld", "heart closed", "divine response blocked", "trespass retained", "will refusing"]

set.analysis_note: The permit-form here carries no forgiveness content; it is used ironically to expose self-righteous blindness—the verse is about moral perception failure, not inner releasing of forgiveness.
Leaving the dead to bury their own dead is a radical call to release prior obligations in favour of discipleship—the inner act is a detachment of will from family duty in response to a higher claim.
Forgiveness is pronounced directly into the inner person of the paralytic in response to faith—sin is released at the soul level, and the word 'take heart' signals the inner restoration that follows.
The rhetorical question probes which act is harder—forgiving sins or healing the body—implying that inner release of sin is the deeper and more authoritative act.
Authority to forgive sins on earth is the inner-being claim being demonstrated through the outward healing—forgiveness is shown to be a real act requiring genuine power over guilt.
Every sin and blasphemy against the Son can be forgiven, but blasphemy against the Spirit cannot—this establishes a limit to forgiveness tied to the inner state of sustained, unrepentable rejection.
Speaking against the Holy Spirit carries permanent unforgiveness across both ages, implying that this inner rejection closes the faculty of repentance itself, making pardon structurally impossible.
set.keywords: ["forgiveness permanent refusal", "spirit rejected", "repentance foreclosed", "hardening final", "pardon impossible"]

set.analysis_note: The instruction to leave the blind guides alone is a volitional release from engagement—the inner act is disengaging will and attention from those whose spiritual blindness makes guidance impossible.
Leaving the ninety-nine to seek the one lost sheep images a volitional prioritising of the strayed—the inner act is releasing the secure many in order to pursue the single vulnerable one.

The question about how often to forgive a sinning brother probes the inner willingness to repeat the act of releasing grievance—the number seven suggests a generous but still countable limit in human imagination.
Pity moves the master to release the servant from his debt entirely—compassion is the inner driver that produces the act of forgiveness, cancelling an obligation that could not be repaid.
The forgiven servant is reminded that his debt was cancelled because he pleaded—the inner act of pleading was the occasion of grace, and forgetting it is the failure the parable condemns.
Forgiveness must arise from the heart—not merely as formal release but as a genuine inner disposition; failure to forgive from the heart disqualifies one from receiving the Father's forgiveness.
Permitting the children to come is an act of welcoming release—the inner content is an open, receptive posture rather than exclusion, modelling the kingdom disposition of welcome.
Peter's statement that they have left everything frames the inner act of release as total—possessions, relationships, and security have all been relinquished in a volitional act of following.

Leaving houses, family, and lands for discipleship is described as a volitional act of total release that earns eschatological reward—the inner act of detachment from all natural bonds is maximally costly.
set.keywords: ["attachment forsaken", "will releasing", "family bonds released", "sacrifice total", "reward eschatological"]

set.analysis_note: Refusing to allow others to enter the kingdom is an act of active obstruction—the inner-being content is a will that blocks others' access, making this a case of permission actively withheld.

Neglecting justice, mercy, and faithfulness while tithing minutely reveals an inner-being failure to prioritise moral weight—the weightier matters of the heart are left undone while external acts are performed.

One is taken and one is left—the verse concerns eschatological separation, not inner forgiveness; the leaving here is a consequential divine act of distinction with no direct inner-being forgiveness content.
Parallel to the preceding verse, one is taken and one left in eschatological separation—no inner forgiveness content is present; the leaving designates consequential division, not relational release.
The shedding of covenant blood is the objective ground by which sins are released; forgiveness is secured through sacrificial death, not inner resolve alone.
The disciples abandoned their teacher in the moment of crisis; this is a failure of loyalty and resolve, not a forgiveness or release in the redemptive sense.
Jesus voluntarily released his spirit at death; the inner act is a willed surrender of life, a final yielding of self.
Repentance is proclaimed as the inner turning that opens the way to forgiveness of sins; the two are inseparably linked as preparation and outcome.
The disciples immediately left their nets to follow; this leaving is a decisive act of will detaching from prior identity and security.
set.keywords: ["will decisive", "attachment severed", "identity released", "devotion whole", "prior life left"]

set.analysis_note: They left family and livelihood behind to follow; the inner act involves releasing relational and economic bonds in response to a call.
set.keywords: ["family bonds released", "will responding", "loyalty transferred", "security abandoned", "call obeyed"]

set.analysis_note: Authority withholds speech from demonic forces; the permit/release here is an exercise of sovereign will that restrains rather than grants freedom.
Seeing their faith, Jesus pronounces sins forgiven; forgiveness is granted in response to trust, releasing the paralytic from the weight of guilt.
The scribes challenge who has authority to forgive sins, insisting only God can release guilt; the verse locates forgiveness as a divine prerogative over the conscience.
Forgiving sins is compared to healing as parallel acts of authority; the verse presents release from guilt as an inner reality that is harder to verify than physical restoration.
The Son of Man claims earthly authority to forgive sins, making forgiveness an act of delegated divine power operative in the present moment.
All sins and blasphemies of humanity fall within the scope of forgiveness; the verse asserts the breadth of divine release covering even severe guilt.
Blaspheming the Holy Spirit places one beyond forgiveness entirely; the inner state is a hardened, permanent guilt that cannot be released.
Forgiveness is withheld from those who do not truly turn; the verse links inner turning with the possibility of release, and the absence of turning with continued guilt.
Permission is withheld from the healed man to accompany Jesus; the release/permit here is an act of divine direction, not a forgiveness or moral inner dynamic.
The religious leaders abandoned God's command in favor of human tradition; the inner act is a willful displacement of divine authority from the conscience.
Tradition is used to block filial duty; permitting someone to fulfill their obligation is refused, blocking the will from acting rightly.
Permission is framed as a sequencing of priorities; the inner dynamic is one of ordered giving, not forgiveness or moral release.
Jesus commands that children be permitted to come; the inner posture is one of open welcome and indignation at exclusion, valuing the vulnerable.
Peter declares that they have left everything to follow; the inner act is a total release of all prior attachments as an expression of devoted will.
Those who have left family and property for the gospel's sake are acknowledged; the inner act is a voluntary severance of all natural bonds driven by higher loyalty.
Jesus refuses to allow the temple to be used as a thoroughfare; this is an exercise of authoritative will to protect sacred space, not a forgiveness act.
Prayer and forgiving others are linked: releasing grievances against others is the inner condition that allows one to receive divine forgiveness in return.
All the disciples abandoned Jesus and fled; the inner act is a failure of loyalty under fear, a collapse of the will to stand by the condemned.
Salvation is specifically defined as knowing that sins are forgiven; forgiveness is the cognitive and experiential heart of what salvation means to the inner person.
Repentance and forgiveness are proclaimed together as a unified message; the inner turning of repentance is the pathway to the release of sins.
Liberation is proclaimed to captives and the oppressed; release here covers both external bondage and the inner state of those burdened by constraint.
The fishermen left everything at the shore to follow Jesus; the inner act is a total voluntary surrender of livelihood and prior life driven by the call.
Seeing their faith, Jesus declares the man's sins forgiven; forgiveness is granted in direct response to trust, releasing the inner burden of guilt.
Religious leaders question who can forgive sins, locating that authority exclusively with God; the verse frames forgiveness as a divine act over the inner moral state.
Declaring sins forgiven is juxtaposed with commanding physical healing; the verse treats forgiveness as an invisible inner release parallel to visible restoration.
The Son of Man exercises earthly authority to forgive sins; inner release from guilt is demonstrated as real by the physical healing that follows.
The verse permits self-examination before judging others; the permit use here is not forgiveness but an ironic call to inner honesty before presuming to correct.
Great love is the evidence of great forgiveness received; the inner experience of being forgiven much produces love, while little forgiveness yields little love.
Jesus directly declares the woman's sins forgiven; the word spoken to her addresses the inner burden of guilt with direct authoritative release.
Onlookers are astonished that Jesus forgives sins, questioning his identity; forgiveness is recognized as a divine act that exceeds human authority.
Jesus tells the man to release the dead to their own obligations and to follow; the inner call is to disengage from competing loyalties and embrace a higher mission.
set.keywords: ["loyalty redirected", "will releasing", "prior claim left", "mission embraced", "attachment severed"]

set.analysis_note: The prayer asks for divine forgiveness linked to the petitioner's own forgiving of debtors; inner release of others' offenses is the stated basis for receiving release oneself.
Speaking against the Son of Man is forgivable, but blaspheming the Holy Spirit is not; there is an inner threshold beyond which the capacity for forgiveness is foreclosed.
The house is declared forsaken; God has left or abandoned a community that rejected him, marking a withdrawal of presence rather than a moral inner act of forgiveness.
Forgiveness is to be granted when a brother repents; the inner act of forgiving is conditioned on the offender's prior turning, making repentance the trigger for release.
Repeated repentance — even seven times in a day — must be met with repeated forgiveness; the will to forgive is called to be persistent and unconditional in its response.
At the end, one is taken and the other left; the leaving here is an eschatological separation with no inner moral or forgiveness content in this verse.
One woman is taken and the other left; as in the parallel verse, this is an eschatological division with no direct inner moral or forgiveness content.
Jesus commands that children be let through to him; the inner posture is one of welcoming openness and rebuke of those who would restrict access.
Peter declares they have left their homes to follow; the inner act is a deliberate severance from domestic security driven by total commitment.
Leaving house, spouse, family for the kingdom is acknowledged as a sacrificial inner act of supreme loyalty to a higher call over all natural bonds.
Jesus prays for the forgiveness of those crucifying him, citing their ignorance; the inner act is a will to forgive even in extremity, grounding release in lack of full knowledge.
Repentance leading to forgiveness of sins is the message to be proclaimed universally; the inner turning and release from guilt are the core content of the mission.
The Father has not left the Son because the Son always does what pleases him; the inner bond of faithfulness and obedience prevents abandonment.
The hired hand, lacking ownership and love, abandons the sheep at the moment of threat — his inner detachment drives desertion where a true shepherd's bond would hold.
In an act of self-giving, permission is granted for the disciples to go free — an inner posture of protective release substituting himself in their place.
Forgiveness of sins is presented as an act that can be extended or withheld — a delegated moral authority over the release of guilt and its retention.
Repentance is the inner turning that unlocks forgiveness of sins — the release of guilt is tied directly to a prior movement of the will away from sin.
set.keywords: ["will turning", "guilt released", "repentance initiating", "conscience cleansed", "sin debt cancelled"]

set.analysis_note: Repentance and turning back are the inner conditions for sins being utterly blotted out — the record of transgression erased from conscience and divine account.
Repentance and forgiveness are paired gifts given from above — neither originates in the human will alone but are granted through divine exaltation of the Savior.
set.keywords: ["repentance gifted", "forgiveness granted", "guilt cancelled", "divine initiative", "sin debt released"]

set.analysis_note: Gamaliel counsels a posture of non-interference — letting events alone — reflecting a will that withholds judgment and permits the situation to resolve itself.
The intent of the heart is named as what requires forgiveness — the inner motive of wickedness is the specific object of the release being sought through prayer.
set.keywords: ["heart intent", "motive wicked", "guilt heart", "will repenting", "forgiveness sought", "conscience implicated"]

set.analysis_note: Belief is the inner act through which forgiveness of sins is received — faith is the sole inner condition for the release of guilt through the name of Christ.
Forgiveness of sins is publicly proclaimed as available through this man — the inner possibility of guilt's cancellation is being announced as news to be received.
Forgiveness is the result of turning from darkness and Satan's power to God — the inner journey from bondage to freedom culminates in the release of guilt.
set.keywords: ["turning inward", "darkness leaving", "guilt released", "will reoriented", "liberation inner", "faith receiving"]

set.analysis_note: Men gave up natural relations — an inner act of wilful abandonment of the created order — and received in themselves the due penalty, showing the self-destructive result of yielding to disordered passion.
set.keywords: ["will abandoning", "passion consuming", "nature forsaken", "desire disordered", "self destruction", "shame internal"]

set.analysis_note: Blessedness is located in having lawless deeds forgiven and sins covered — the inner state of the one whose guilt has been lifted is declared a state of deep wellbeing.
set.keywords: ["guilt lifted", "soul blessed", "lawlessness forgiven", "sin covered", "conscience freed", "wellbeing inner"]

set.analysis_note: While still enemies, the inner posture of enmity toward God was overcome by Christ's death — reconciliation transforms a hostile relationship into one that enables ongoing salvation.
The husband is instructed not to dismiss or abandon his wife — the will to dissolve the marriage bond is restrained, and reconciliation is presented as the preferred inner posture.
If separation has occurred, the inner aim should be restoration of the marital relationship — reconciliation to the husband is named as the rightful goal of the separated wife.
A believer married to an unbeliever who consents to stay should not be abandoned — the will to maintain the bond is upheld against the impulse to dissolve it.
A woman is instructed not to send away a consenting unbelieving husband — the will to abandon the relationship is restrained in favour of maintaining the bond.
God initiates reconciliation through Christ, drawing humanity out of alienation into restored relationship — and then entrusts participants with the ongoing ministry of that reconciling work.
God's reconciling of the world involves not counting trespasses against people — the inner account of transgression is suspended as the basis for restored standing.
set.keywords: ["trespass uncounted", "guilt suspended", "enmity dissolved", "world reconciled", "alienation reversed", "account cleared"]

set.analysis_note: The appeal 'be reconciled to God' calls for a human inner response to divine initiative — the will is summoned to abandon enmity and receive the restored relationship being offered.
Forgiveness of trespasses flows from redemption through blood, grounded in the riches of grace — guilt is released not by inner effort but by the abundance of divine generosity.
set.keywords: ["guilt released", "trespass forgiven", "grace abundant", "redemption received", "sin debt cancelled"]

set.analysis_note: The cross is the means by which deep relational hostility is killed — reconciliation here involves the total elimination of an inner and structural enmity between estranged parties.
Redemption is defined as the forgiveness of sins — the inner reality of being freed from guilt is identified as the core content of what redemption means.
Through the blood of the cross, all things are to be reconciled to God — a cosmic restoration of right relationship between creation and Creator, healing universal alienation.
Reconciliation through Christ's bodily death aims at presenting the person holy, blameless, and above reproach — the inner and moral transformation is the purpose of restored standing.
The written record of legal debt and its demands standing against humanity is cancelled and nailed to the cross — the objective burden of guilt is completely erased.
set.keywords: ["debt cancelled", "guilt record erased", "legal demand removed", "condemnation lifted", "conscience freed"]

set.analysis_note: Believers are urged to leave behind the elementary foundation, including repentance from dead works — the inner posture of advancing faith requires releasing prior rudimentary stages.
set.keywords: ["will advancing", "foundation left", "spiritual progress", "repentance foundational", "inner movement forward"]

set.analysis_note: Without blood there is no forgiveness — the release of guilt requires atoning sacrifice; the inner reality of forgiveness is inseparable from the objective act of atonement.
set.keywords: ["guilt requiring atonement", "sin requiring blood", "forgiveness conditioned", "atonement necessary", "conscience purified"]

set.analysis_note: Where forgiveness is fully achieved, no further sacrifice is needed — the completeness of guilt's release means the inner conscience no longer requires repeated ritual cleansing.
set.keywords: ["guilt fully released", "atonement complete", "conscience settled", "offering ended", "forgiveness final"]

set.analysis_note: The prayer of faith not only brings physical healing but also releases any committed sins — faith expressed in intercession opens the door to forgiveness of guilt.
set.keywords: ["faith interceding", "sin released", "guilt forgiven", "prayer efficacious", "healing forgiveness linked"]

set.analysis_note: Confession is the inner act that unlocks forgiveness and cleansing — the will's honest acknowledgment of sin triggers the divine faithfulness that releases guilt and purifies conscience.
set.keywords: ["confession activating", "guilt released", "conscience cleansed", "will honest", "sin acknowledged", "unrighteousness purged"]

set.analysis_note: The assurance that sins are already forgiven through the name of Christ grounds the inner identity and standing of the believer — guilt is already released, not conditionally pending.
set.keywords: ["guilt already released", "assurance inner", "identity grounded", "sin forgiven", "conscience settled"]

set.analysis_note: The community has abandoned its first love — an inner withdrawal of the heart's devoted affection from its original fervour, leaving behind what once motivated it.
set.keywords: ["love abandoned", "heart withdrawn", "affection forsaken", "devotion cooling", "will drifting", "first love lost"]

set.analysis_note: God will wipe away every tear — the eschatological erasure of all grief and suffering from the inner experience of the redeemed, leaving no residue of sorrow.
set.keywords: ["grief erased", "sorrow removed", "suffering ended", "inner pain wiped", "consolation complete", "tears eliminated"]

set.analysis_note: People from all nations refuse to allow the bodies to be buried — the inner posture is one of defiant withholding of even the basic dignity of burial, indicating deep-seated hostility.
Every tear, mourning, crying, and pain will be completely wiped away — the total erasure of all inner suffering and grief is the final condition of the redeemed in the new creation.
set.keywords: ["suffering erased", "mourning removed", "pain eliminated", "grief wiped", "inner restoration complete", "sorrow ended"]
